title: In-Place Radix Sort: Incorrect Digit Selection

statement.markdown:
Radix sort is a non-comparative integer sorting algorithm. It works by grouping numbers by individual digits starting from the least significant digit. Implement an efficient in-place radix sort function for integers.

The function should sort the array in ascending order, and the input array is guaranteed to contain only non-negative integers.

However, the given implementation has a bug in selecting the least significant non-zero digit for radix sort. Fix the bug to make the function work correctly.
statement.inputFormat: A non-empty list of non-negative integers.
statement.outputFormat: The sorted list of non-negative integers in ascending order.
statement.notes: You can assume the input array will contain integers within the range 0 to 2^31 - 1.

examples.explanation: Radix sort groups the input elements in a way that the output elements are sorted in ascending order.

hints.text: First hint text - Review the implementation of finding the least significant non-zero digit

editorial.title: Editorial Title
editorial.intuition: Explanation of the correct approach - Identify the radix sort algorithm's core mechanism and its application to integers.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach - Radix sort implementation without optimizations.
editorial.optimalSolution: Optimal solution details - Efficient in-place radix sort implementation with least significant non-zero digit selection.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Correctness proof - Proof that the optimal solution correctly sorts the input array.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run - A detailed example of the optimal solution's execution.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly identifying the radix sort algorithm's core mechanism
Insufficient analysis of the least significant non-zero digit
